rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute urinary retention with 14 hours of absolute anuria, a grossly distended painful bladder and overflow dribbling requires urgent (within hours) bladder decompression by catheterisation — a procedure not available via telehealth or reliably at a standard GP visit. The destination is an emergency department (or an equivalent after-hours/urgent service able to catheterise) today: T4. It is not routinely a call-000 ambulance emergency (T5) because it is not immediately life-threatening in minutes and the patient can usually self-transport — but the tier escalates to T5 if he cannot get to care because of pain or being alone, or if features of infected obstruction/urosepsis (fever, rigors, flank pain) or collapse develop.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Ongoing absolute inability to pass urine with severe suprapubic pain and distension
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Get to an emergency department / urgent service able to catheterise within hours — do not wait until the morning
escalation_edges.rationale: Retention needs same-day decompression to relieve pain and prevent post-renal acute kidney injury; delay risks worsening pain and upper-tract damage.
escalation_edges.communication_template: You have a blocked bladder that needs a small tube (catheter) passed to drain it. Please get to an emergency department now — today, not in the morning. If you can't get there because of the pain or because you're on your own, call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, shaking chills (rigors) or flank/back pain — possible infected obstruction / urosepsis
escalation_edges.rationale: An obstructed, infected urinary tract can progress to septic shock within hours and is a surgical/urological emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever, shaking chills, or pain in your back or sides, call 000 straight away — that can mean a dangerous infection behind the blockage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to get to an emergency department because of pain or being alone with no transport
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 now if self-transport is not possible
escalation_edges.rationale: The patient lives alone; if pain or isolation prevents safe self-transport, an ambulance is the appropriate route to timely decompression.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain is too much to travel or there's no one to take you and you can't get there safely, call 000 for an ambulance.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: After catheterisation: catheter stops draining, bladder pain returns, or the patient cannot void after any future catheter removal
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Return to emergency care promptly if drainage stops or retention recurs
escalation_edges.rationale: A blocked or non-draining catheter re-creates the obstruction; recurrence of retention needs re-catheterisation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the catheter stops draining or the pain comes back, go back to the emergency department — the bladder must not be left blocked.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one available to help or transport him
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Explicitly check he can get to care safely; if not, escalate to a 000 ambulance rather than assume self-transport, and ensure he can unlock the door for help.
Higher risk of significant retained volume and post-obstructive complications; counsel first-dose postural hypotension when an alpha-blocker is later started.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You have a blocked bladder (acute urinary retention) that needs to be drained with a catheter today — please go to an emergency department now. Do not wait for the morning clinic, and do not force lots of fluids to try to flush it while you still can't pass urine. If you can't get there safely, or if you develop a fever, shaking chills or back pain, call 000.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Emergency department today (self-transport if safe); call 000 if unable to travel or if warning signs develop
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Emergency departments are open 24 hours — this must be dealt with tonight, not deferred to a daytime appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the ED/urgent service with the history of 14-hour retention, the 6-month obstructive symptoms, and the recent OTC decongestant. Arrange GP follow-up in 3–5 days for a trial without catheter and to start prostate treatment.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating acute retention as a routine or next-day problem (e.g. book a GP appointment, wait until morning) instead of arranging same-day decompression.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Prolonged high-pressure retention causes post-renal acute kidney injury, hydronephrosis, severe unrelieved pain and, rarely, bladder-wall injury.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Same-day GP/urgent care that cannot reliably catheterise, delaying definitive decompression by hours of additional retention and pain.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Defaulting every patient to a 000 ambulance when safe self-transport to ED is available and no sepsis/collapse features are present — unnecessary ambulance use, low clinical harm but not the routine baseline.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether an infection is precipitating or complicating the retention, and the true retained volume and renal impact, cannot be determined via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate for in-person decompression and testing regardless, and safety-net explicitly for fever/rigors/flank pain and for recurrence.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Delaying escalation to 'see if it settles', trialling antibiotics for a presumed UTI, or advising increased fluids while the patient remains in retention.
